title.emphasis: Part II: The Condemnation of Humanism and Kantian Ethics: The Tyranny of the Universal

section.para: The attempt to construct a "universal" ethics based on the flawed normative subject does not lead to universal flourishing. Instead, it inevitably becomes a tool for exclusion and oppression, creating a social reality in which those who do not conform to the idealized norm are legally, socially, and philosophically devalued. Humanism and its Kantian philosophical antecedents, despite their claims to enlightened reason and progress, are the primary architects of this normative cage. Their universalism is not a genuine embrace of all humanity but the universalization of a specific, exclusionary, and ultimately violent ideal.

The series of Humanist Manifestos published throughout the 20th and 21st centuries present a worldview grounded in reason and scientific inquiry, directed toward the goal of human flourishing.1 While claiming a progressive lineage, these documents codify a destructive ideology of "productivism" or "growthism"—the belief that measurable productivity and economic growth constitute the purpose of human organization.2 This constructs a social reality that devalues any existence outside of productive labor and provides a philosophical justification for planetary-scale ecological devastation.

A close reading of the Humanist Manifestos reveals this underlying logic. Humanist Manifesto II (1973), for example, declares that "Using technology wisely, we can control our environment, conquer poverty, markedly reduce disease... [and] alter the course of human evolution".1 On its surface, this is a call for progress. However, this vision of progress is predicated on a productivist logic that is causally linked to ecological collapse. The relentless pursuit of growth necessitates endless resource extraction and habitat destruction, placing humanity in the position of an unprecedented "global superpredator".2 The current "biological annihilation" that characterizes the Sixth Mass Extinction is not an accident but the material consequence of the successful implementation of this humanist vision of mastery over nature.2 The philosophy's profound anthropocentrism, which reduces the entire non-human world to a resource for human exploitation, is a direct intellectual descendant of the colonial person/property binary identified in Part I.2

This productivist logic does not only harm the non-human world; it is also the engine of social exclusion. The ideology of productivism requires a specific kind of body: one that is efficient, mobile, and replaceable.2 This gives rise to a system of "compulsory able-bodiedness," the unspoken assumption that the able-bodied individual is the natural, normal, and required state of being.2 This economic imperative is enshrined as a fundamental right in Article 23 of the UDHR, which states, "Everyone has the right to work".2 From a crip theory perspective, this "right" functions as a normative command. It ties human value and full participation in society directly to productive labor, implicitly devaluing and marginalizing anyone who cannot or chooses not to engage in wage work, including the disabled, the elderly, children, and caregivers.2 Humanism's vision of progress is not neutral; it is progress

towards a specific, normative ideal of the productive, able-bodied worker. This project of social construction necessarily devalues and marginalizes anyone who does not fit the ideal and destroys the non-human world in the process. The philosophy is therefore an active agent of harm.

title.emphasis: The Kantian Subject as an Engine of Exclusion

section.para: The philosophical DNA for Humanism's idealized subject can be traced to the ethics of Immanuel Kant. The Kantian demand for a purely rational, autonomous agent as the basis for moral worth is a philosophical abstraction that is scientifically untenable and has been historically used as a legal justification for dehumanization and the denial of rights.

Kant's categorical imperative famously requires that one "treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end".9 The moral transgression to be avoided is instrumentalization, or objectification.9 However, the framework is condemned by its own definition of "humanity," which Kant equates with an individual's rational nature and capacity for autonomous choice.9 This establishes an exclusionary ideal as the prerequisite for moral consideration.

This ideal is a neurological fiction. Modern neuroscience and psychology have demonstrated that human cognition is not a process of pure, disembodied reason. Decision-making is deeply intertwined with emotion, unconscious processes, environmental cues, and biological imperatives.6 The purely rational agent, free from the influence of its facticity, does not exist. The Kantian subject is an impossible abstraction.

The historical and legal consequences of this abstraction have been devastating. The legal requirement of "rationality" has been consistently weaponized to deny personhood and rights to those deemed "irrational." This logic was used to justify colonialism, slavery, and the disenfranchisement of women, all of whom were framed as lacking the requisite rational capacity for self-governance. This exclusionary logic persists in modern legal instruments. The Council of Europe's Oviedo Convention, for example, contains controversial articles pertaining to persons "not able to consent," which authorize interventions without consent for individuals with mental or intellectual disabilities.2 These articles stand in stark contradiction to the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (CRPD), which champions the right to legal capacity on an equal basis with others.2 The Oviedo framework is a modern legal echo of the Kantian error, perpetuating ableism by prioritizing a normative standard of cognitive capacity and justifying the violation of bodily autonomy for those who deviate from that standard. The Kantian universal, therefore, is a false one; it is a standard that, by its very definition, excludes a vast portion of humanity from the sphere of full moral and legal recognition.
